title: Human Development Index groups
subtitle: The Human Development Index (HDI) is a summary measure of key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, a good education, and a decent standard of living. The groups below define different levels of human development depending on the HDI values.
caption:
Human Development Index groups, 2023

The Human Development Index (HDI) is a summary measure of key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, a good education, and a decent standard of living. The groups below define different levels of human development depending on the HDI values.

This choropleth map displays the 2023 Human Development Index (HDI) classification for countries worldwide, using a four-tier color scale to represent development levels. The legend defines the categories as Low (dark red), Medium (yellow-orange), High (light blue), and Very High (dark blue), with a hatched pattern indicating areas where no data is available. The highest development tier, Very High, covers most of North America, including the United States and Canada, as well as the majority of Western and Northern Europe, such as the United Kingdom, France, Germany, and the Nordic countries. Australia, New Zealand, Japan, and South Korea also fall into this dark blue category. The High development group, shown in light blue, includes much of Latin America, such as Brazil, Argentina, and Mexico, alongside nations like Russia, China, and Saudi Arabia. The Medium tier, colored yellow-orange, encompasses a broad band of countries across South and Southeast Asia, including India and Indonesia, as well as parts of North Africa and the Middle East. The Low development category, marked in dark red, is concentrated primarily in sub-Saharan Africa, covering nations such as Niger, Chad, the Central African Republic, and Somalia. Greenland is the only major landmass shown with the hatched "No data" pattern. The geographic distribution shows a clear concentration of the highest development levels in the Global North and Oceania, while the lowest levels are clustered in central and western Africa.
